nativeRange.habitat: The Queen Triggerfish inhabits rocky and coral reef habitats, typically at depths of 3–30 m, though individuals have been recorded as deep as 275 m. Adults are sometimes observed schooling over adjacent sand and seagrass flats.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices for shelter
robust rockwork that the fish cannot easily rearrange
no fragile decor or unsecured equipment

diet.notes: Requires a varied meaty diet to mirror its natural feeding on benthic invertebrates. Mussels, clams, shrimp, krill, squid, and chopped fish are commonly recommended several times per day. Hard-shelled foods (whole crustaceans, snail shells) are generally advised to help wear down the continuously growing teeth and reduce the risk of dental overgrowth.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 3862x2575. Sharp full-body in-situ reef shot showing the whole fish with its elongated dorsal and caudal filaments, blue curved face lines and teal body — best complete photograph.

Diego Delso, 5626x3751. Tack-sharp head close-up showing the diagnostic bright blue lines radiating around the eye and mouth, orange throat and purple body; tight crop so kept as a detail extra, not the primary.
Bernard DUPONT, 3151x2080. Clean full side profile of a different individual with vivid orange throat, blue face bands and fin filaments; adds angle/individual variety.

summary: A large, brightly patterned Atlantic and Caribbean triggerfish prized for its bold personality and vivid coloration. Widely considered aggressive and not safe for reef systems, the Queen Triggerfish is best kept singly as the centerpiece of a spacious fish-only or FOWLR aquarium.
careNotes:
The Queen Triggerfish (Balistes vetula) is a large, intelligent, and notoriously bold Atlantic triggerfish that has been reported to reach up to about 60 cm (24 in) in total length, though most captive specimens settle in around 30–40 cm. Lifespans in well-kept aquariums tend to be 7–15 years; wild individuals have been documented surviving over 20 years.

A spacious system is generally advised — most experienced sources recommend roughly 500 US gallons (about 1,900 L) for a full-grown adult, with a tank footprint that emphasizes length and open swimming space. Smaller volumes are sometimes used successfully but tend to amplify aggression and can stunt long-term growth.

Water chemistry is standard tropical reef: temperature around 22.5–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, specific gravity 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12. The species is broadly considered hardy and generally tolerant of minor parameter swings once acclimated.

Queen Triggerfish are not considered reef-safe. They will readily consume crustaceans (shrimp, crabs, lobsters), molluscs, sea urchins, and starfish, and may nip or destroy corals and sessile invertebrates — both as targeted prey and incidentally through their habit of biting and rearranging tank decor.

Temperament is widely regarded as aggressive. They have been known to redecorate aquascapes, attack heaters, powerheads, and airline tubing, and bite hands and equipment during maintenance — particularly as they mature. They are best added LAST to a tank and generally kept as the smallest of their tankmates so they do not dominate other large fish. Suitable tankmates tend to be robust and similarly assertive: large angelfish, moray eels, large wrasses, and surgeonfish of similar size are commonly compatible. Small, peaceful, or slow-moving fish, other triggerfish, and crustaceans are best avoided.

Diet is carnivorous and should be varied and protein-rich, with regular inclusion of hard-shelled foods such as whole shrimp, clams in shell, mussels, and similar items to wear down the continuously growing teeth — overgrown teeth can prevent feeding and become a husbandry emergency.

The species possesses a robust dorsal-fin 'trigger' mechanism that locks the first spine erect, and powerful, beak-like jaws capable of inflicting painful bites; care should be taken during handling, netting, and any hands-in-tank work. The Queen Triggerfish is not venomous, and its skin and flesh are not known to be toxic to other tank inhabitants (though wild specimens occasionally accumulate ciguatera toxin and are generally not considered safe for human consumption from affected regions).

The species is currently assessed as Near Threatened by the IUCN; most aquarium-trade specimens are wild-caught from the Caribbean.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been meaningfully documented. In the wild, Queen Triggerfish are demersal spawners — the female digs a shallow nest in sand and aggressively guards the eggs until hatching. Reproduction in aquaria is considered highly unlikely given the tank volumes, pairing dynamics, and feeding requirements involved.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name (Linnaeus, 1758), taxonomy (Balistidae / Tetraodontiformes), max length 60 cm TL, wild temperature range 22.5–28°C, depth 2–275 m (usual 3–30 m), native Western and Eastern Atlantic distribution, trophic level 3.8 (benthic-invertebrate feeder), maximum age ~23 years, and IUCN status Near Threatened.

additional.name: AquariumSource
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (500 gal recommended; some hobbyists keep in 200–300 gal), water parameters (matching LiveAquaria), lifespan (7 yr avg, up to 13 yr in captivity), diet (mussels, squid, krill, shrimp, fish, sea urchins; hard-shell foods for dental health), reef compatibility (no), and temperament. Designated secondaries Reef2Reef wiki (403) and Fishkeeping World (404) failed; AquariumSource used as additional secondary.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 60 cm, temperature 74–79°F (23–26°C), aggressive temperament, carnivorous diet (squid, shrimp, mussels, fish), and that conspecifics and small fish should be avoided.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive-breeding records have been documented. In the wild, the Queen Triggerfish is a demersal spawner — the female excavates a shallow nest in sand and guards the eggs aggressively until they hatch. The tank volumes, pairing dynamics, and territory requirements involved make reproduction in home aquaria effectively unreported.
